title: Precision limits for time-dependent quantum metrology under Markovian noise
abstract: We derive ultimate precision bounds for estimating parameters encoded in \emph{time-dependent} Hamiltonians in the presence of general Markovian noise, allowing for arbitrary adaptive protocols with fast controls and noiseless ancillas. Extending the minimization-over-purifications framework to time-varying continuous channels, we obtain a differential upper bound on the achievable quantum Fisher information (QFI) that can be evaluated at all times via semidefinite programming. For parameter-independent noise, we prove a universal long-time scaling law: if the coherent (noiseless) dynamics yields $Q_{\mathrm{coh}}(T)\sim T^{2k}$, then under Markovian noise the QFI scales at most as $Q(T)\sim T^{2k}$ in the DHNLS regime, whereas in the DHLS regime it is fundamentally limited to $Q(T)\sim T^{2k-1}$. We illustrate these behaviors on paradigmatic driven-qubit sensors, exhibiting $T^{4}$ and $T^{3}$ scalings under dephasing and spontaneous emission, respectively. Finally, we provide explicit continuous exact and approximate quantum error correction constructions -- supplemented by spin-squeezed probes -- that asymptotically saturate the bounds, establishing their tightness.
summary: This paper establishes the fundamental precision limits for quantum metrology when using time-dependent Hamiltonians under Markovian noise. It identifies two distinct regimes where the noise either preserves or reduces the scaling of the Quantum Fisher Information. The authors prove these bounds are achievable using continuous quantum error correction and spin-squeezed probes.
main_problem: Determining the ultimate precision limits (Quantum Fisher Information) for estimating parameters in time-dependent Hamiltonians subject to Markovian noise.
main_result: The authors derive a differential upper bound on the QFI and prove a universal scaling law: in the DHNLS regime, the noiseless scaling is preserved, while in the DHLS regime, the scaling exponent is reduced by exactly one.
method: The study uses an extension of the minimization-over-purifications framework to time-varying continuous channels, evaluated via semidefinite programming (SDP), and demonstrates achievability through continuous and approximate quantum error correction (QEC).
model_or_system: The model involves quantum probes governed by the GKSL (Lindblad) master equation, specifically examining amplitude-modulated and rotating field Hamiltonians under dephasing and spontaneous emission noise.
key_observables: Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) for frequency estimation.
important_parameters: DHNLS (Derivative of the Hamiltonian Not in Lindblad Span) and DHLS (Derivative of the Hamiltonian in Lindblad Span) regimes; long-time scaling laws (T^2k vs T^{2k-1}).
main_assumptions: The noise model is assumed to be Markovian and independent of the applied time-dependent control Hamiltonians.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a pictorial representation of the general adaptive strategy involving a probe, time-dependent channels, unitary controls, and noiseless ancillas.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of time-dependent metrology under noise, derives a differential upper bound using a modified purification framework, establishes asymptotic scaling laws for different noise regimes, and concludes by proving the tightness of these bounds via specific QEC and spin-squeezing protocols.
why_interesting: It provides fundamental limits for quantum sensing in realistic, noisy environments and shows that the metrological advantages of time-dependent modulation can be preserved using advanced error correction strategies.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Pictorial representation of the most general adaptive strategy considered in this Letter. A probe evolves with a channel Λi ω and then a unitary control is Ci applied, coupling the probe to any number of noise- less ancillas. This step can be repeated an arbitrary number of times N. Notice that, differently from [24], the channels could be different each step, Λi ω ̸= Λj ω, which is equivalent to consider a time-dependent chan- nel.

FIG. 2. Plot of the full numerically optimized bound (solid blue) and its asymptotic behaviors (dashed lines) for B = 1, ω = 1, ε = 0.1. Panel a) shows HAC under dephasing along the x direction, while the inset depicts spontaneous emission noise. Panel b) shows HRF under dephasing along the x direction, while the inset depicts spontaneous emission noise.

FIG. 3. a) Pictorial representation of the QEC scheme to achieve the bound Eq. (4) in the DHNLS scenario. A QEC step is applied periodically, coupling the probe to a noiseless ancilla, the effective dynamics becomes a unitary U(ω, t) as discussed in the main text. b) Pictorial representation of the AQEC scheme to achieve the bound Eq. (4) in the DHLS scenario. An AQEC step is applied periodically, coupling the probe to a N noiseless ancillas, the effective dynamics becomes a channel Σ(ω, t), whose effect in logical space is described by Eq. (16).
